description:
PROBLEM
  internal/modelcatalog/manifest.go carries the legacy v4 schema with overlapping concepts: profiles AND targets AND aliases AND surface_policy AND ModelEntry.Tier back-pointers AND compatibility_target redirection. The v0.11 surface redesign collapses this to a single first-class concept (policy) and drops everything else.

ROOT CAUSE
  Manifest accumulated migration debt from prior renames. compatibility_target/target duality is half-done rename cruft (validator at manifest.go:460 says they must match when both set). surface_policy was an attempt to centralize per-(target × harness) defaults that ages poorly once reasoning_default moves to per-model. ModelEntry.Tier is a back-pointer to a target that no longer exists in v0.11.

DESIGN INTENT (v0.11)
  - Top-level keys: models, policies, providers (catalog-side type+include_by_default declarations), sampling_profiles (UNCHANGED, ADR-007).
  - REMOVED: targets:, aliases:, compatibility_target on policies, surface_policy blocks, ModelEntry.Tier field.
  - Each policy: { min_power: int, max_power: int, allow_local: bool, require: []string }.
  - Hard cut on schema version (v4 → v5). External manifests pinned to v4 fail at load with helpful error pointing at ADR-009 (Phase 8).
  - No aliases. The four canonical policy names (default, cheap, smart, air-gapped) are the only valid values. code-economy/code-smart/standard/local/offline/fast/code-fast/code-high/code-medium are removed with no replacement.
  - Validator requires catalog to define a "default" policy (fallback for empty req.Policy).
  - New BillingForProviderSystem and BillingForHarness functions in internal/modelcatalog/billing.go: hardcoded mapping by provider/harness type (lmstudio/llama-server/omlx/vllm/rapid-mlx/ollama/lucebox → fixed; openai/openrouter/anthropic/google → per_token; claude/codex/gemini harnesses → subscription; native fiz harness derives from underlying provider).

CURRENT STATE WITH FILE:LINE
  - internal/modelcatalog/manifest.go:36-76 ModelEntry (with Tier field at line 40)
  - internal/modelcatalog/manifest.go:107-118 manifest struct (Targets, Profiles fields)
  - internal/modelcatalog/manifest.go:120-126 profileEntry
  - internal/modelcatalog/manifest.go:128-218 surfaceValue/targetEntry/surfacePolicyEntry types (~91 lines to delete)
  - internal/modelcatalog/manifest.go:245-487 loadManifest + validateManifest
  - internal/modelcatalog/manifest.go:489-545 upgradeManifest (delete entirely)
  - internal/modelcatalog/manifest.go:577 profileCompatibilityTarget
  - internal/modelcatalog/catalog.go:36,53-69 Catalog struct, Profile/ResolvedTarget types
  - internal/modelcatalog/catalog.go:122-182 Profiles/Profile/Aliases methods

PROPOSED FIX
  1. Drop ModelEntry.Tier field. Add ModelEntry.ReasoningDefault (reasoning.Reasoning type) for per-model intrinsic reasoning posture.
  2. manifest struct: drop Targets, drop Profiles. Add Policies map[string]policyEntry, Providers map[string]providerEntry. No top-level aliases field.
  3. policyEntry: { MinPower int, MaxPower int, AllowLocal bool, Require []string }. providerEntry: { Type string, IncludeByDefault bool, Billing string (optional, required if Type unknown) }.
  4. Delete surfaceValue, targetEntry, surfacePolicyEntry types (~91 lines).
  5. Rewrite validateManifest: max_schema_version = 5; every policy has min<=max valid power range; every provider has type; "default" policy must exist; require[] entries must be in known invariant set ({"no_remote"}); user-config providers with Type not in known catalog set must declare Billing explicitly.
  6. Delete upgradeManifest; v4→v5 is a hard cut. Loader emits "manifest schema v5 required (got v%d); see ADR-009 for v0.11 schema redesign" on version mismatch.
  7. Catalog struct: rename profileEntries → policyEntries. Replace Profile struct with Policy{Name string, MinPower, MaxPower int, AllowLocal bool, Require []string}. Drop ResolvedTarget, SurfacePolicy, MissingSurfaceError, DeprecatedTargetError, UnknownTargetError, Surface enum, ResolveOptions, Resolve, Current, AllConcreteModels, CandidatesFor, AllModelsInTier, TierModel, concreteModelsForSurface, primaryConcreteModel, targetCandidateIDs, deprecatedRoutingGuidance.
  8. Catalog: add Policy(name) (Policy, bool), Policies() []Policy. Drop Aliases() entirely (no aliases concept).
  9. New file internal/modelcatalog/billing.go: BillingModel enum (fixed/per_token/subscription) + BillingForProviderSystem(string) BillingModel + BillingForHarness(string) BillingModel.
  10. Inline-YAML test fixtures in internal/modelcatalog/manifest_test.go and catalog_test.go: rewrite from legacy v4 schema to v5 in lockstep with the production code change.

NON-SCOPE
  - Catalog YAML rewrite (internal/modelcatalog/catalog/models.yaml) — Phase 3.
  - Public service surface — Phase 4.
  - Provider config (user-side) — Phase 5.
  - Specs — Phase 8.

PARENT + DEPS
  Depends on Phase 1 (uses new error types).

description:
P0 critical-path: the translator changes shipped in 7870cc84 wire the
catalog reasoning_wire correctly for OR-Qwen3, but two dialect bugs
remain that block sindri and ds4 lanes:

1. **sindri-llamacpp Qwen3 thinking does not activate.** Verified by the
   2026-05-11 probe artifact under docs/research/probe-reasoning-2026-05-
   11/sindri/: all six matrix rows returned 0 reasoning_tokens. Direct
   probing showed that top-level `enable_thinking`/`thinking_budget` is
   silently dropped by llama-server. The chat_template_kwargs envelope
   works: 250-526 completion tokens with extracted reasoning_content.

2. **ds4 deepseek-v4-flash thinking is uncontrolled.** ds4's
   /v1/chat/completions accepts only flat top-level `reasoning_effort`
   (verified via /props.api.supported_request_parameters); the
   Anthropic-shape `thinking: {type, budget_tokens}` that fizeau emits
   today via ThinkingWireFormatThinkingMap is silently ignored. ds4
   /props.reasoning.aliases declares {low→high, medium→high, xhigh→high};
   only `max` is genuinely distinct (and requires --ctx ≥ 393216, which
   vidar:1236 satisfies).

Implementation:

### 1. Add ThinkingWireFormatOpenAIEffort constant

In internal/provider/openai/protocol_support.go:
  ThinkingWireFormatOpenAIEffort ThinkingWireFormat = "openai_effort"

Wire shape: flat top-level `reasoning_effort: \"<tier>\"`. Disable form:
top-level `think: false` (per ds4 docs).

### 2. Fix ThinkingWireFormatQwen to emit chat_template_kwargs envelope

In internal/provider/openai/openai.go (around line 421-425 today after
7870cc84), replace:
  option.WithJSONSet(\"enable_thinking\", true)
  option.WithJSONSet(\"thinking_budget\", thinkingBudget)
with:
  option.WithJSONSet(\"chat_template_kwargs\", map[string]interface{}{
      \"enable_thinking\": true,
      \"thinking_budget\": thinkingBudget,
  })

Off-path (around openai.go:391-394) similarly:
  chat_template_kwargs.enable_thinking = false

Per-request `reasoning_format` is OPTIONAL — sindri's server-side default
is `auto` which extracts thinking correctly. v1 leaves it unset.

### 3. Add OpenAIEffort branch in reasoningRequestOptions

In internal/provider/openai/openai.go, add a case for
ThinkingWireFormatOpenAIEffort in both the explicit-off path and the
explicit-on path:
  - off: option.WithJSONSet(\"think\", false)
  - explicit named tier: option.WithJSONSet(\"reasoning_effort\",
    string(tier))
  - tokens budget on an effort wire: snap via PortableBudgets nearest
    tier (NearestTierForTokens shipped in 7870cc84 already)

### 4. Switch ds4 provider's static ThinkingFormat

In internal/provider/ds4/ds4.go:
  ProtocolCapabilities.ThinkingFormat = ThinkingWireFormatOpenAIEffort

Today it's set to Thinking:true with no explicit ThinkingFormat (or
inherited ThinkingMap default — verify in code). Change to OpenAIEffort
explicitly.

### 5. Tests

internal/provider/openai/openai_test.go:
  - Qwen wire: reasoning=4096 emits chat_template_kwargs envelope, NOT
    top-level enable_thinking
  - Qwen wire off: reasoning=off emits
    chat_template_kwargs.enable_thinking=false
  - OpenAIEffort wire: reasoning=high emits reasoning_effort:\"high\"
  - OpenAIEffort wire off: reasoning=off emits think:false
  - OpenAIEffort wire with tokens budget: reasoning=4096 emits
    reasoning_effort:\"medium\" (snap via NearestTierForTokens)
  - Recorded-fixture regression: byte-identical wire output for
    OR + ThinkingMap paths (no change).

internal/provider/ds4/ds4.go assertions:
  - ProtocolCapabilities.Thinking == true
  - ProtocolCapabilities.ThinkingFormat == OpenAIEffort

### 6. Profile comments

Refresh comments in:
- scripts/benchmark/profiles/vidar-ds4.yaml — note ds4 alias collapse;
  only `high` and `max` are practically distinct
- scripts/benchmark/profiles/sindri-club-3090-llamacpp.yaml — note that
  thinking_budget is a soft template hint that doesn't bind; max_tokens
  caps actual

In-scope files:
- internal/provider/openai/openai.go
- internal/provider/openai/protocol_support.go
- internal/provider/openai/openai_test.go
- internal/provider/ds4/ds4.go
- scripts/benchmark/profiles/vidar-ds4.yaml (comments only)
- scripts/benchmark/profiles/sindri-club-3090-llamacpp.yaml (comments only)

Out-of-scope:
- ds4 catalog entry creation (filed separately in the verify+populate
  bead).
- Live verification against vidar/sindri (filed separately).
- Reasoning-token extraction from reasoning_content (fizeau-8f62bcbb).
- Introspection adapters (fizeau-e80c156e).

description:
PROBLEM
DDx pre-claim readiness needs a "smart/frontier" route, but Fizeau's public routing surface currently exposes overlapping concepts (`profile`, `model_ref`, `model`, `min_power`, `max_power`, and `harness`) with unclear semantics. DDx temporarily switched readiness to `model_ref=smart` in v0.6.2-alpha40 because the installed Fizeau route-status path reported no smart route even though GPT-5.5 was available. That workaround is not an acceptable long-term contract; human input is needed on the Fizeau-side routing API.

USER FEEDBACK
The operator explicitly rejected the current workaround: "model_ref is bullshit". Treat this bead as a design/contract decision gate, not a request to deepen DDx's workaround.

OBSERVED EVIDENCE ON 2026-05-08
- Installed binary: `/home/erik/go/bin/fiz` reports `fiz v0.10.13`.
- Installed binary command:
  `fiz route-status --profile smart --json --work-dir /Users/erik/Projects/ddx`
  returned `candidates: []` and error `no live provider supports prompt of 0 tokens with tools=false at tier >= smart`.
- The same installed catalog advertises smart entries: `fiz catalog show` reports `agent.openai: gpt-5.5` and `agent.anthropic: opus-4.7` under `smart`.
- DDx proved GPT-5.5 was reachable through the workaround path: after installing DDx v0.6.2-alpha40, `ddx run --model-ref smart --harness codex --text 'Reply exactly: alpha40-smart-ok' --timeout 45s` succeeded and session `as-199166d6` recorded `harness=codex`, `model=gpt-5.5`.
- Current Fizeau checkout differs from the installed binary: `./fiz --version` reports `v0.10.12-0.20260506230320-1819c0a582c2+dirty`; `./fiz route-status --profile smart --json --work-dir /Users/erik/Projects/ddx` now returns smart candidates including `claude/opus-4.7` and `agent/openrouter openai/gpt-5.5`.
- In the same checkout, `./fiz route-status --model-ref smart --harness codex --json --work-dir /Users/erik/Projects/ddx` returns `candidates: []` and the same `no live provider ... tier >= smart` error because the codex candidate is currently unhealthy/quota-limited. This shows the surface is also sensitive to quota/health state in a way that produces misleading operator language.

CODE ANCHORS
- `service.go:368-378` defines `RouteRequest` with `Profile`, `Model`, `Provider`, `Harness`, `ModelRef`, `MinPower`, and `MaxPower` side by side.
- `service.go:676-688` defines `ServiceExecuteRequest` with both `ModelRef` and `Profile` exposed publicly.
- `service_routing.go:46-82` threads profile, model-ref, provider preference, and power bounds into internal routing.
- `service_routing.go:1091-1115` maps profile to provider preference.
- `service_routing.go:1117-1150` derives profile power policy but explicitly does not apply it when `Model` or `ModelRef` is set.
- `service_execute.go:296-305` treats `smart`/`code-smart`/`code-high` as subscription-only explicit profile constraints when a harness is pinned.
- `agentcli/routing_smart.go:823-825` exposes both `--profile` and `--model-ref` on `route-status`, making users choose between two smart-ish abstractions.

IMPACT
- Downstream callers can report "no smart model available" while GPT-5.5 is actually reachable through a different Fizeau field.
- DDx readiness can park, skip, or fail-open beads for the wrong reason.
- Operators are forced to know that `model_ref=smart` may work when `profile=smart` does not, which is not a coherent public API.
- Quota/health rejection is currently collapsed into a generic "no live provider" message that hides the existence of smart candidates and the reason they were rejected.

HUMAN DECISION NEEDED
Decide the canonical Fizeau contract for "smart/frontier route": should callers express it as `profile=smart`, numeric power (`min_power` / `max_power`), a catalog alias, or a new intent field? Decide whether `ModelRef` should remain public, become internal, be renamed, or be deprecated. Decide how explicit harness pins interact with smart intent and how route-status/Execute should explain quota/health rejection.

PROPOSED WORK AFTER DECISION

- Align `RouteRequest`, `ServiceExecuteRequest`, `route-status`, `Execute`, and CLI help with that contract.
- Add tests for the chosen behavior, especially smart intent with and without explicit `--harness codex`.
- File or update downstream DDx cleanup work to remove the `model_ref=smart` workaround once Fizeau exposes the final surface.

NON-SCOPE

notes:
DECISION RECORDED 2026-05-08

Canonical Fizeau routing-surface semantics for v0.11 (decided via design conversation):

PROFILE → POLICY (rename)
  "Policy" is the single canonical word. No aliases (code-economy/code-smart/standard/local/offline/fast/code-fast/code-high/code-medium are dropped with no replacement). Four canonical policies:
    default:    {min_power: 7, max_power: 8, allow_local: true}
    cheap:      {min_power: 5, max_power: 5, allow_local: true}
    smart:      {min_power: 9, max_power: 10, allow_local: false}
    air-gapped: {min_power: 5, max_power: 5, allow_local: true, require: [no_remote]}

MODELREF → REMOVED ENTIRELY
  Hard cut at v0.11.0. No compat period, no deprecated alias. Operators using --model-ref get a cobra "unknown flag" error. ServiceExecuteRequest.ModelRef field is deleted (not deprecated). Catalog ref expansion previously done via ModelRef no longer happens — callers pass concrete Model when pinning, otherwise rely on Policy.

MIN_POWER / MAX_POWER → SOFT SCORING SIGNALS
  Fields preserved but no longer enforced as filters. Scorer applies asymmetric penalties: undershoot 5x worse than overshoot. A power-7 candidate beats a power-11 candidate when both healthy and policy is min:8/max:10. Smart's allow_local: false is what produces fail-closed behavior when subscription is dry, NOT a hard power floor.

HARNESS PINS
  Pin overrides provider.include_by_default (operator can reach excluded providers via pin). Pin does NOT override policy.require[] (--policy air-gapped --provider openrouter fails with ErrPolicyRequirementUnsatisfied). Pin-pin conflicts (harness×model, harness×provider, provider×model) fail with ErrUnsatisfiablePin.

ERROR LANGUAGE FOR THE FOUR ROUTE-STATUS SCENARIOS

  --policy smart (subscription healthy):
    Returns smart candidates from claude/codex/gemini subscription harnesses.

  --policy smart (subscription dry):
    Returns ErrNoLiveProvider with RejectedCandidates populated. Error message: "policy 'smart' has N candidate(s) matching power 9-10 but all rejected: Q quota-exhausted, U unhealthy".

  --policy smart --harness codex (codex healthy):
    Pin to codex; returns the gpt-5.5 candidate.

  --policy smart --harness codex (codex unhealthy/quota-exhausted):
    Returns ErrNoLiveProvider with RejectedCandidates=[{Harness: codex, Reason: quota_exhausted}]. Error message names the specific harness and reason.

  --model-ref smart --harness codex (transition):
    Cobra "unknown flag: --model-ref" error. No transition period.

  Execute({Policy:"smart", Harness:"codex"}):
    Same resolution as --policy smart --harness codex above. Field is Policy not Profile (Go compile error if caller uses Profile).

IMPLEMENTATION BEADS (FILED)

  Phase 1: fizeau-b633cbb3 — internal/routing rename + soft min/max + ModelRef removal + ErrNoLiveProvider quota/health distinction (this addresses the operator-visible error language ask in AC4 of this bead)
  Phase 2: fizeau-040f1f83 — manifest schema redesign + billing.go
  Phase 3: fizeau-0aaa94e5 — catalog/models.yaml v5 rewrite
  Phase 4: fizeau-c6c8bc9e — public service surface
  Phase 5: fizeau-2d0bd0a4 — provider config (include_by_default + Billing)
  Phase 6: fizeau-399ecb48 — CLI surface (fiz policies + fiz harnesses subcommands; --model-ref/--profile flags removed)
  Phase 7: fizeau-9eb62a07 — internal/core ModelRef removal + telemetry/session-log schema break
  Phase 8: fizeau-264e70c4 — ADR-009 + CONTRACT-003 + FEAT-003/004/005/006 rewrites (this is where the canonical semantics get committed to a Fizeau design document, satisfying AC1)
  Phase 9: fizeau-32e533bb — DDx coordination (drop --model-ref, rename Profile→Policy in DDx call sites; satisfies AC5's downstream cleanup)

AC SATISFACTION TABLE

  AC1 (semantics in design doc) → Bead 8 produces ADR-009.
  AC2 (covers Profile/ModelRef/Model/MinPower/MaxPower/Harness) → all named above; ADR-009 documents each.
  AC3 (ModelRef fate + compat period) → removed entirely, no compat. Beads 1/4/5/6/7/9 implement; Bead 8 documents.
  AC4 (route-status + Execute behavior) → Beads 1, 4, 6; ErrNoLiveProvider enhancement in Bead 1 specifically addresses the "operator-visible error language" concern.
  AC5 (follow-up implementation beads) → the nine beads above.
  AC6 (go test not required) → N/A; decision-only.

This bead can be closed by the human reviewer once the decision above is acceptable. Implementation tracks via the nine listed beads.

description:
Make the openai-compat provider's reasoning translator consult the
L1→L2→L3 wire-form chain from ADR-010 Amendment 2026-05-11:

  L1: live introspection (from the introspection-adapter bead
      fizeau-e80c156e — read once at provider construction, merged into
      ProtocolCapabilities)
  L2: catalog `reasoning_wire` field (from the schema bead
      fizeau-1567453f)
  L3: static ThinkingFormat per provider type (today's behavior)

Per the amendment, this bead also fixes two pre-existing translator bugs
discovered in the 2026-05-11 probing session and adds one new wire format.

Three coordinated changes:

### 1. L1/L2/L3 lookup chain

Signature change: `openRouterReasoningOptions(policy)` becomes
`openRouterReasoningOptions(policy, model, effectiveWire)`. The
`reasoningRequestOptions` caller (around openai.go:340) consults in
order:
  - introspection.SupportedRequestParams + introspection.AliasMap (L1)
  - modelReasoningWireFor(model) (L2)
  - p.thinkingWireFormat() default (L3)
and passes the resolved wire choice into format-specific helpers.

Tier↔tokens conversion via PortableBudgets:
  - caller passed KindNamed, wire wants tokens: PortableBudgets[name]
  - caller passed KindTokens, wire wants tier: snap to nearest
    PortableBudgets tier rounding UP on ties (4096 -> medium because
    4096 is the midpoint of 2048+8192).

### 2. Fix ThinkingWireFormatQwen to use chat_template_kwargs envelope

Verified 2026-05-11 against sindri-llamacpp: top-level enable_thinking
is silently dropped (4 completion tokens, no reasoning). The
chat_template_kwargs envelope variant produces 250-526 tokens with
reasoning extracted.

The current code at openai.go:421-425 emits:
  option.WithJSONSet(\"enable_thinking\", true)
  option.WithJSONSet(\"thinking_budget\", thinkingBudget)

The new code must emit:
  option.WithJSONSet(\"chat_template_kwargs\",
      map[string]interface{}{
          \"enable_thinking\": true,
          \"thinking_budget\": thinkingBudget,
      })

Off-path (openai.go:391-394) similarly moves from top-level to kwargs:
  chat_template_kwargs.enable_thinking = false

The per-request `reasoning_format` override is OPTIONAL — sindri's
server-side default is `auto` which handles extraction correctly. Only
emit `reasoning_format` if introspection (L1) reported a different
server-side default and we want to override. v1 leaves the field
unset; revisit if needed.

### 3. Add ThinkingWireFormatOpenAIEffort for ds4

New constant in
`internal/provider/openai/protocol_support.go`:
  ThinkingWireFormatOpenAIEffort ThinkingWireFormat = \"openai_effort\"

Wire shape: flat top-level `reasoning_effort: \"<tier>\"`. Disable form:
top-level `think: false` (per ds4 docs, not `reasoning_effort: \"none\"`).

In openai.go reasoningRequestOptions add branches for OpenAIEffort:
  - off: option.WithJSONSet(\"think\", false)
  - explicit named tier: option.WithJSONSet(\"reasoning_effort\", string(tier))
  - tokens budget on an effort wire: snap via PortableBudgets nearest tier

Provider capability change: at `internal/provider/ds4/ds4.go` set
`ProtocolCapabilities.ThinkingFormat = ThinkingWireFormatOpenAIEffort`.
This is the static L3 default; L1 introspection will confirm at
construction time.

### Test plan

Update internal/provider/openai/openai_test.go with:
  - reasoning=low + wire=tokens (L2) emits `reasoning.max_tokens: 2048`
  - reasoning=4096 + wire=effort (L2) emits `reasoning.effort: \"medium\"`
    (round-up rule on tie)
  - reasoning=high + wire=effort (L2) emits `reasoning.effort: \"high\"`
  - reasoning=high + wire=provider (L3 fallback) emits today's
    `effort: \"high\"` (backwards compat regression check)
  - reasoning=low + wire=effort + ds4 alias map (L1) emits
    `reasoning_effort: \"high\"` (low aliases to high per /props)
  - Qwen wire: reasoning=4096 emits chat_template_kwargs envelope, NOT
    top-level
  - Qwen wire off: reasoning=off emits chat_template_kwargs.enable_thinking
    = false
  - ds4 wire off: reasoning=off emits think:false (not reasoning_effort)
  - Recorded-fixture regression: byte-identical wire output for the
    pre-amendment OR + Qwen + ThinkingMap paths when wire=\"\" (L3 only).

Add a structured-warning log assertion when L2 overrides L1 (mirrors
ADR-006 override-as-failure-signal pattern).

In-scope files:
- internal/provider/openai/openai.go (translator + helper)
- internal/provider/openai/protocol_support.go (add OpenAIEffort constant)
- internal/provider/openai/openai_test.go
- internal/provider/ds4/ds4.go (switch ThinkingFormat to OpenAIEffort)
- internal/provider/llamaserver/llamaserver.go (audit: should already be
  Qwen format; verify caps unchanged)
- internal/reasoning/reasoning.go (expose BudgetForNamed +
  NearestTierForTokens helpers if not already public)
- internal/reasoning/reasoning_test.go

Depends on: fizeau-1567453f (schema), fizeau-e80c156e (introspection).

Out-of-scope:
- Surface-id vs catalog-id lookup fix (lands in populate bead together
  with catalog value updates).
- vLLM caps re-audit (defer).

acceptance: Both child beads (preflight smoke test + matrix runner consecutive-failure halt) are closed. A test sweep launched against a deliberately misconfigured lane (e.g. profile with sampling param the provider rejects) terminates in <60s with a clear error naming the lane and the failing parameter, instead of writing N cells of identical failures.
description:
Two-failure pattern from 2026-05-10 sweeps where a single misconfigured lane wasted significant compute before any human noticed:

(1) sindri-llamacpp lane: profile had `reasoning: low` set, but fiz's llama-server provider rejects that param. Every cell in the lane failed in ~47s with the same `agent: provider error: openai: reasoning="low" is not supported by provider type "llama-server"` error. The matrix runner kept marching through all 89 tasks × 3 reps without bailing. Caught after 119 cells (~93 minutes of churn).

(2) sindri-vllm lane (same evening): the vLLM model server entered a degraded state where /v1/models responded but /v1/chat/completions hung indefinitely. Each cell wasted its full agent budget (~45 min) waiting for a single llm.request that never produced an llm.response.

Common shape: same misconfiguration / failure causing every cell in a lane to fail identically. Today there's no mechanism to detect "this lane is not going to work, stop trying."

Two child beads address this in priority order:

- [primary] preflight smoke test — one HTTP completion per lane before any matrix runner spawns, fails the sweep with a clear diagnostic if any lane 's endpoint can't produce 1 token in 30s with the lane's sampling params. Catches both failure modes above plus auth/missing-model/connection-refused at startup, with O(N_lanes) cost not O(N_lanes × N_cells).

- [defense in depth] matrix runner consecutive-failure halt — track identical-fingerprint failures and abort the lane with an explicit `lane_aborted` status after N=5 in a row. Catches mid-sweep regressions where preflight passed but the endpoint dies later.

Out of scope: the more invasive fiz-side approach (distinct EX_CONFIG exit code) — lower bang for buck since it still wastes one cell per lane and the request-shape vs transient classification is fragile.
notes: Review 2026-05-12 acceptance re-read: closable. The epic acceptance is the fail-fast behavior: preflight catches a deliberately misconfigured lane before cell fanout, and the matrix runner halts lanes after consecutive identical failures. Verified scripts/benchmark/test_preflight_lane.bash passed, and go test ./cmd/bench -run 'TestConsecutiveFailureHalt|TestPreflight|TestSweepShutdownTermGraceful' -count=1 passed. The open child fizeau-b76d6a2c is a separate shutdown/reaping reliability issue; its current shell fixture waits on a fake Harbor startup log and is too timing-sensitive to be a blocker for this fail-fast epic.
title: EPIC: bench — fail fast on misconfigured/dead lanes

acceptance: 1. New fixture graded-fail-retry-spam.json + test asserts invalid_provider on turns=6 + input_tokens=0 + output_tokens=0 + had_llm_request=true + tmw=nil. 2. reclassify-invalid.py --dry-run flags the 70+ historical vidar 2026-05-11 cells as transitioning to invalid_provider. 3. After apply, those cells are excluded from vidar's pass-rate denominator. 4. Subsequent sweep with --retry-invalid re-runs them when the endpoint is back up.
description:
fiz retries transient provider errors (connection refused, EOF, 5xx) internally up to ~5 times with exponential backoff. Each retry attempt counts as a turn in the matrix, inflating the turns counter even when zero useful work happened. Result: cells where the provider was completely unreachable end up with turns=6 (5 retries), output_tokens=0, terminated_mid_work=nil, but `matrixHasMeaningfulAttempt(report)` returns true because turns > 0 — bypassing the new invalid_provider classifier rule introduced in 6688326e (fizeau-e874633d).

Concrete incident: on 2026-05-11 ~15:25Z, vidar:1235 oMLX server crashed. 70 cells silently failed before detection. fiz.txt showed:
  2026/05/11 15:29:41 WARN provider error, retrying attempt=1 err="unexpected EOF"
  2026/05/11 15:29:42 WARN provider error, retrying attempt=2 err="connect: connection refused"
  ... etc through attempt=5
Each cell ended with turns=6, wall=60-200s, final_status=graded_fail. Classifier left them as plain graded_fail — i.e. "the model attempted and failed" — polluting vidar's pass-rate denominator with what was actually 100% provider unavailability.

The hole is structural: hasMeaningfulAttempt currently looks at turns/tokens/tool_calls > 0. fiz's retry counter is one of the inputs to that signal, but a retry isn't a meaningful attempt — it's the absence of one.

PROPOSED FIX

Add a stronger "no real attempt" signal that distinguishes retry-spam from real turns. Two complementary options:

A. **fiz records retry attempts separately** from successful llm.request/response pairs. The session jsonl would carry an `llm.retry` event (or annotate llm.request with an `attempt` field). The matrix runner already reads the session jsonl in readSessionSignalsFromCell — it can extend the count to distinguish "had_completed_response" from "had_request". Then: graded_fail + !had_completed_response + had_request → invalid_provider, regardless of turns count.

B. **Token-based fallback in the existing classifier**: even if turns > 0, if input_tokens=0 AND output_tokens=0 AND had_llm_request=true AND tmw=nil, classify as invalid_provider. The intuition: a real turn consumes prompt tokens (input_tokens > 0 from session.end usage). All-zero token counts means no completed round-trip ever happened. Cheaper than (A) but a bit more heuristic.

In-scope files for Part B (simpler, ships first):
- cmd/bench/matrix_invalid.go — extend the case "graded_fail" branch with the token-based check before the !hasAttempt branch.
- cmd/bench/matrix_invalid_test.go — fixture: graded-fail-retry-spam.json with turns=6, input_tokens=0, output_tokens=0, had_llm_request=true, tmw=nil → expects invalid_provider.
- scripts/benchmark/reclassify-invalid.py — mirror.
- Run reclassifier; expect ~70+ historical vidar cells from 2026-05-11 to flip invalid_setup/empty → invalid_provider.

In-scope for Part A (deeper, file as separate bead if simple fix isn't enough):
- internal/core/loop.go — distinguish "request attempted but provider error retried" from "completed request/response pair" in event emission. Add an `attempt` field on llm.request or a new event type.
- scripts/benchmark/harbor_agent.py FizeauAgent.parse_telemetry — count completed responses (not just requests) when populating turns/tokens fields.
- cmd/bench/matrix.go readSessionSignalsFromCell — return n_completed_responses alongside the existing tmw/had_llm_request signals.

ACCEPTANCE IS A COMMAND

1. go test ./cmd/bench/... -run TestClassifyMatrixInvalid passes with the new fixture graded-fail-retry-spam.json asserting invalid_provider.
2. .venv-report/bin/python scripts/benchmark/reclassify-invalid.py --dry-run flags the cells from the 2026-05-11 vidar incident (70+ cells) as transitioning to invalid_provider.
3. Apply pass leaves those cells excluded from vidar's pass-rate denominator and tagged for --retry-invalid reruns.

WHY THIS MATTERS

Without this, every server-side outage (oMLX crash, vLLM hang, network blip) gets attributed to model quality instead of provider availability. Pass@k denominators include hundreds of cells where the model never actually got to try. The defense-in-depth piece (fizeau-72d18004 consecutive-failure halt in the matrix runner) catches this at sweep-execution time, but classification matters even retroactively for accurate reporting.
title: bench: classifier misses retry-spam cells (turns inflated by fiz internal retries on connection errors)

description:
PROBLEM
  v0.11 is a breaking redesign of the routing surface (Profile→Policy, drop ModelRef, drop targets, drop aliases, schema v5, billing classification, default-deny pay-per-token via include_by_default). The governing specifications (CONTRACT-003, FEAT-003/004/005/006, AGENTS.md, CHANGELOG, docs/routing/) currently describe the v0.10 surface and would mislead any reader after v0.11 lands. There is no ADR documenting the redesign decision.

ROOT CAUSE
  Specs accumulate during normal feature work. A breaking redesign of this magnitude needs a single ADR that captures the decision context and consequences, plus a coordinated rewrite of the touched specs so reviewers can assess whether the code matches the intent.

DESIGN INTENT (v0.11)
  - New artifact: ADR-009-routing-surface-redesign.md documenting the rename, removal, and schema decisions. Cross-references and partially supersedes ADR-005, ADR-006. Does not affect ADR-007 (sampling profiles unchanged).
  - Full rewrite: CONTRACT-003-fizeau-service.md to reflect the new FizeauService shape (~14 methods, PolicyInfo type, dropped ResolveProfile/ProfileAliases/ModelRef).
  - Full rewrite: FEAT-004-model-routing.md (policies, soft min/max, allow_local, require[], pin precedence rules).

description:
CONTEXT
The routing engine already scores with power, cost, quota, locality, utilization, performance, context headroom, cooldown, and sticky affinity. SD-005 names the public evidence components as `power_weighted_capability`, `power_hint_fit`, `latency_weight`, `placement_bonus`, `quota_bonus`, `marginal_cost_penalty`, `availability_penalty`, and `stale_signal_penalty`. Today those facts are present indirectly through buckets such as `power`, `cost`, `quota_health`, and `performance`, which makes route explanations harder to compare to the design.

CHANGE
Align public route candidate score component evidence with SD-005 vocabulary while preserving existing route-order behavior unless a test explicitly documents a deliberate change. Keep typed filter reasons separate from scoring. Power hints must remain asymmetric: being below `MinPower` is penalized more strongly than being above `MaxPower`.

IN-SCOPE FILES
- internal/routing/score.go
- internal/routing/*score*_test.go or routing preference tests
- service_routing.go candidate component mapping
- service_events.go if event JSON mirrors component names

OUT-OF-SCOPE
- Snapshot assembly.
- Rewriting eligibility gates.
- Changing public request field names.
title: routing: align score components with SD-005 evidence names

description:
PROBLEM
  internal/config/config.go user-side ProviderConfig has no include_by_default field, has DefaultModelRef in RoutingConfig, has ModelRef in BackendPoolConfig and ProviderOverrides, and routes through a model-ref → catalog.Resolve pipeline that the v0.11 surface no longer supports.

ROOT CAUSE
  Config schema accreted ModelRef threading when model-ref was the canonical routing input. With ModelRef removed (Phase 4), those fields are dead. Per-provider include_by_default is the v0.11 mechanism for default-deny on per-token providers (Phase 2 catalog ships the defaults; user config overrides per-provider).

DESIGN INTENT (v0.11)
  - ProviderConfig gains IncludeByDefault *bool. Pointer so unset means "use catalog default"; set means "override catalog default for this user".
  - User config CAN add provider entries with Type values not in the catalog's known set, but MUST declare Billing explicitly (per_token | subscription | fixed). Catalog-known types inherit billing from BillingForProviderSystem.
  - DefaultModelRef field in RoutingConfig: REMOVED. ModelRef field in BackendPoolConfig and ProviderOverrides: REMOVED.
  - Concrete-model selection chain after this phase: overrides.Model → BackendPool.Model → ProviderConfig.Model. No catalog-ref expansion in the config layer.

CURRENT STATE WITH FILE:LINE
  - internal/config/config.go:38-68 ProviderConfig (no IncludeByDefault, no Billing)
  - internal/config/config.go:140-178 RoutingConfig (DefaultModelRef field at line 147)
  - internal/config/config.go:182-193 BackendPoolConfig (ModelRef at line 185)
  - internal/config/config.go:196-200 ProviderOverrides (ModelRef at line 198)
  - internal/config/config.go:686-720, 728-740, 837-870 ResolveProviderConfig, BuildProviderWithOverrides, ResolveBackend (model-ref → catalog.Resolve pipeline)

PROPOSED FIX
  1. ProviderConfig: add IncludeByDefault *bool yaml:"include_by_default,omitempty"; add Billing string yaml:"billing,omitempty" (validated: required when Type unknown to catalog).
  2. RoutingConfig: drop DefaultModelRef field.
  3. BackendPoolConfig: drop ModelRef field.
  4. ProviderOverrides: drop ModelRef field.
  5. Rewrite ResolveProviderConfig, BuildProviderWithOverrides, ResolveBackend: drop the modelref→catalog.Resolve path; concrete model selection becomes overrides.Model → bc.Model → pc.Model with no catalog expansion.
  6. Catalog stays in scope only for cat.LookupModel(id) lookups for power/billing/context-window enrichment.
  7. Effective IncludeByDefault resolution: if user-config value set (pointer non-nil), use it; else use catalog default (cat.Provider(name).IncludeByDefault).
  8. Validator: if user-config provider's Type is not in catalog's known set AND Billing is empty, fail with "provider %q: type %q is not catalog-known; declare billing field explicitly (per_token | subscription | fixed)".

NON-SCOPE
  - Catalog YAML providers block — Phase 3.
  - Public service surface — Phase 4.
  - CLI flags — Phase 6.

PARENT + DEPS
  Depends on Phase 2 (catalog Provider type/methods).

description:
Collect volatile per-provider runtime signals (status, quota
remaining, recent p50 latency) and write them to the runtime tier of
M1's cache. Consumed by M2's snapshot assembler at read time to set
KnownModel.Status, .QuotaRemaining, .RecentP50Latency.

## Public API sketch

```go
package runtimesignals

type Signal struct {
    Provider          string
    Status            modelregistry.ModelStatus
    QuotaRemaining    *int        // nil when unknown
    QuotaResetAt      *time.Time
    RecentP50Latency  time.Duration
    LastSuccessAt     *time.Time
    LastErrorAt       *time.Time
    LastErrorMsg      string
    RecordedAt        time.Time
}

func Collect(ctx context.Context, providerName string, cfg *config.ProviderConfig) (Signal, error)
```

## Sources per provider type

- **openrouter, openai-compat**: parse rate-limit headers from any
  recent response (already plumbed via commit 7776890e
  `quota_exhausted`); fetch `/usage` if available
- **claude-subscription**: existing `quota_cache.go` code in
  `internal/harnesses/claude/`; expose its data through this
  surface
- **codex-subscription**: same for `internal/harnesses/codex/quota_cache.go`
- **gemini-subscription**: same for `internal/harnesses/gemini/quota_cache.go`
- **local providers (ds4, llama-server, omlx)**: status only (alive
  check via /v1/models or /props ping); no quota concept

## Aggregation

p50 latency comes from a sliding-window in-memory counter (recent N
requests per provider). On crash/restart, signal is reset (cache file
is empty until next collection). Acceptable — runtime signals are
short-TTL by design.

## Cache integration

Each provider gets its own `runtime/<provider>.json` file in M1's
cache. 5-minute TTL. Stale-while-revalidate: snapshot reads return
immediately, background refresh fires.

## In-scope files

- internal/runtimesignals/signal.go
- internal/runtimesignals/collect.go — per-provider-type collectors
- internal/runtimesignals/headers.go — rate-limit header parsing
  (extends commit 7776890e)
- internal/runtimesignals/latency.go — sliding-window p50
- internal/runtimesignals/signal_test.go
- internal/runtimesignals/collect_test.go

## Depends on

- M0 (ADR-012) accepted
- M1 (cache infrastructure)
- Existing `internal/harnesses/{claude,codex,gemini}/quota_cache.go`
  (consumed, not modified — this bead exposes their data uniformly)

## Out of scope

- Snapshot assembly (M2).
- CLI (M4).
- Persistent historical metrics (out-of-scope per ADR-012; this is
  current-state only).

description:
Follow-on epic for the approved routing plan from 2026-05-13.

End state: every unpinned autorouting decision is a pure function of client inputs plus the current fiz models snapshot. The snapshot is the single source of routing facts: health, quota, model availability, context/tools/reasoning support, billing kind, effective cost, actual-cash-spend flag, locality, reliability, latency, utilization, and freshness. Route code must not maintain a second hidden provider-probe path.

Freshness is a fiz contract, not a required fiz daemon. Fiz provides synchronous refresh APIs/CLI behavior with cross-process locks, single-flight/coalescing, TTLs, cooldowns, and atomic snapshot writes. Autorouting can synchronously refresh stale routing-relevant fields before scoring. CLI commands must not create refresh storms. fiz models is quick and reads the snapshot; if stale fields exist it prints clear refresh guidance rather than launching uncoordinated background probes. Foreground --refresh and --refresh-all are available for serverless operation.

Fiz also exposes an optional async refresh/warmup mechanism that an external long-lived owner such as ddx server can call on a heartbeat to maintain freshness. That async maintainer is an optimization only; route correctness must not depend on ddx server integration.

Routing gates only on true constraints or dispatchability: pins, no_remote, metered actual-spend refusal, required tools/context/reasoning, known down endpoints, quota exhaustion, and pin conflicts. Everything else is scoring. Subscription-backed models use PAYG-equivalent shadow/effective cost for scoring, with actual_cash_spend=false, so cheap naturally prefers nano/mini/local over GPT-5.5 without policy-specific exclusion gates.

Closes when child beads specify, implement, and verify: specs, snapshot schema, coordinated refresh, CLI refresh behavior, routing purity, effective-cost scoring, and route traceability.

description:
Add `fiz models` as a top-level CLI subcommand. Default view shows
the assembled snapshot from M2; flags filter and refresh. Detail view
via `fiz models <ref>` with shortform fuzzy resolution per ADR-012's
naming convention.

Also updates FEAT-006 (standalone CLI) doc to register the new
subcommand and document the interface.

## CLI surface

```
fiz models [flags]                     # list view
fiz models <ref> [flags]               # detail view
fiz cache prune                        # related cache GC
```

## Flags (list view)

- `--refresh`         Force-refresh all sources synchronously before showing
- `--no-refresh`      Don't trigger background refresh on stale data
- `--json`            Machine-readable output
- `--provider <name>` Filter to one provider
- `--power-min <n>`   Filter Power >= n
- `--power-max <n>`   Filter Power <= n
- `--include-noise`   Show low-power, unranked, deprecated entries
  (default suppresses to keep output clean)

## Default filter rules (suppress noise)

- Within (family, tier): keep top 2 versions (current + previous)
- Drop entries with Power < 3 (noise)
- Drop entries with Tier == TierUnknown AND Power == 0
- Show AutoRoutable=false but mark them clearly

`--include-noise` shows everything (useful for debugging the
snapshot, OR's long tail, etc.)

## Detail view (`fiz models <ref>`)

Resolution per ADR-012:
- Full canonical (`<provider>/<id>`) → exact lookup
- Shortform (no `/`) → search snapshot:
  - Exactly one match: use it
  - Multiple matches: list candidates with full canonical IDs, exit 1
  - No matches: show fuzzy-match suggestions, exit 1

Detail output: full KnownModel struct + raw discovery data + full
catalog entry + recent runtime signals + AutoRoutable composition
breakdown (which factor blocked, if any).

## Output format (default)

Tabular, columns: PROVIDER MODEL FAMILY VERSION TIER POWER COST/M
STATUS QUOTA AUTO

Color: AutoRoutable=true green, =false dim. Pre-release in italics.
Status colored (Available green, RateLimited yellow, Unreachable red).

`--json` emits the snapshot serialized as JSON.

## In-scope files

- cmd/fiz/models.go — subcommand registration
- cmd/fiz/models_list.go — list view + filters
- cmd/fiz/models_detail.go — detail view + resolution
- cmd/fiz/models_test.go
- cmd/fiz/cache_prune.go — `fiz cache prune` subcommand
- docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-006-standalone-cli.md — register
  `fiz models` and `fiz cache prune` subcommands; document flags

## Depends on

- M0 (ADR-012) accepted
- M2 (snapshot assembler)
- M3 (runtime signals)

## Out of scope

- Routing's consumption of the snapshot (handled in routing-layer
  beads downstream; this bead is the operator UI)
- OR sub-provider syntax in resolution (M5 deferred)

description:
Add a per-lane preflight check to scripts/benchmark/run_terminalbench_2_1_sweep.sh that fires a single 1-token chat completion against each requested lane's endpoint before any matrix runner spawns. If the endpoint cannot produce a token within 30s using the lane's actual sampling params, fail the sweep with a non-zero exit and a clear message naming the lane, the endpoint, the model id, and the underlying error.

Why this matters: see parent epic. The 2026-05-10 sindri-llamacpp incident wasted 119 cells (~93 min) on a `reasoning: low` parameter the llama-server provider rejects. The same hour, sindri-vllm wasted multiple cells on a hung model engine where /v1/models responded but /v1/chat/completions never streamed tokens. A single curl-to-completion check per lane catches all these classes (plus auth failures, missing-model 404s, connection-refused, wrong content-type) at sweep startup, before any matrix runner spawns.

Behavior:

- Fire after `Resolved tasks:` is printed, before the per-lane "starting" markers.
- For each lane in the resolved plan: build a JSON body using the lane's `fizeau_env.FIZEAU_MODEL` and the lane's sampling block (temperature, top_p, top_k, reasoning when present). POST to `<base_url>/chat/completions` with `max_tokens: 1` and a 30s timeout.
- Pass condition: the response is JSON-parseable and `.choices[0].message.content` is a non-null string (even an empty string is OK — the model started decoding).
- Fail condition: any non-2xx, timeout, malformed JSON, or missing message content. Print the lane id, base_url, model id, sampling params attempted, and the response body (truncated to 1KB).
- Skip preflight for managed-cloud lanes that have always-on SLAs (openrouter, openai, anthropic) — these are well-known and a preflight just burns quota. Identify by checking the resource group's `provider_type` against an allow-list (vllm, llama-server, omlx, lmstudio, rapid-mlx, ollama, lucebox).

In-scope files:
- scripts/benchmark/run_terminalbench_2_1_sweep.sh — add preflight_lane() function + per-lane invocation loop right before the existing per-lane plan output.
- scripts/benchmark/terminalbench-2-1-sweep.yaml — no schema change needed; the existing `fizeau_env` and `sampling` blocks are sufficient.

Out-of-scope (file separate beads):
- Pre-resume validation that the model server is the same version as last time (different problem; would need version stamps on cells).
- Per-cell health probe during the sweep (defense-in-depth piece is fizeau-XXXX, the consecutive-failure halt bead).
- Adding the smoke check to non-TerminalBench benchmark scripts.

Acceptance is a command:

1. With sindri-llamacpp configured to use `reasoning: low` (the broken state we just fixed), `scripts/benchmark/run_terminalbench_2_1_sweep.sh --phase full --lanes sindri-llamacpp,vidar --dry-run` exits non-zero with `[preflight] sindri-llamacpp FAILED: reasoning="low" is not supported by provider type "llama-server"` (or equivalent error text) and does not print any per-lane "starting" marker.
2. With sindri-llamacpp correctly configured (no `reasoning` param), the same command prints `[preflight] sindri-llamacpp OK (1 token in <wall>s)` and continues to the lane plan output normally.
3. Test by killing the llama-server process: `scripts/benchmark/run_terminalbench_2_1_sweep.sh --phase full --lanes sindri-llamacpp` exits non-zero with `[preflight] sindri-llamacpp FAILED: connection refused` (or curl error 7) and no matrix runner is spawned.
4. The cloud lane skip works: `scripts/benchmark/run_terminalbench_2_1_sweep.sh --phase full --lanes openrouter-qwen36 --dry-run` does not call openrouter and prints `[preflight] openrouter-qwen36 SKIPPED (managed cloud lane)`.

Cost budget: at most ~30s × N_lanes added to sweep startup wall time. For an 11-lane full sweep that's ~5 min, much cheaper than waiting for the first cell of each broken lane to fail.

description:
Refactor the TB-2.1 sweep plan schema so lanes and task-subsets are orthogonal. Today scripts/benchmark/terminalbench-2-1-sweep.yaml couples them: each phase carries a task subset AND a curated lane bundle, and each lane lists which phases it's enrolled in. The runner errors when --lanes intersected with selected phase(s) yields empty (cmd/bench/sweep.go:773 "none of --lanes ... are present in selected phase(s)"). Today's incident: `--lanes fiz-sindri-lucebox-qwen3-6-27b --phase all` aborted because `all` iterates phases lucebox isn't enrolled in.

description:
Design ADR (no implementation in this bead) for cost-based routing
where quota-remaining is a factor in the cost score. Implementation
follows in a separate EPIC after the ADR is accepted.

Per operator direction 2026-05-11:

> For a given model power, we need to intelligently choose based on
> "cost", where remaining usage quota impacts the cost.

Cost model (proposed for ADR-011):

- **Subscription with quota remaining**: effective cost ≈ 0 (or
  quota-fraction-aware: lower-cost the more quota remains).
- **Subscription with quota exhausted**: drop from candidates.
- **Per-token API**: effective cost = catalog `cost_input_per_m` ×
  estimated input tokens + `cost_output_per_m` × estimated output
  tokens.
- **Local provider** (free): effective cost = 0 if eligible (per bead
  D's IncludeByDefault filter), else dropped.

Quota signal sources:

- HTTP rate-limit response headers (per recent commit 7776890e —
  `quota_exhausted` already plumbed).
- Subscription `/usage` endpoints when available.
- In-memory recent-rejection rate as fallback.

Quota-pool semantic (from bead E1):

- Models declare a `quota_pool` (defaulting to provider system).
- Pool exhaustion drops all models in that pool from the candidate set.
- Models in a different pool (e.g., `gpt-5.3-codex-spark` in
  `openai-codex-spark`) become eligible as fallbacks even if not
  version-newest, satisfying the "use spark when main pool is empty"
  operator request.

Power-floor semantic:

- Caller / DDx specifies required `MinPower` (and optional `MaxPower`).
- Routing filters candidates to `MinPower ≤ candidate.power ≤ MaxPower`.
- Among survivors, pick min effective cost.
- Tie-break: prefer subscription over PAYG; prefer lower-power if it
  exceeds requirement (cheapest qualified candidate, not most powerful).

## ADR scope

The ADR document itself, written to
`docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-011-cost-based-routing-with-quota-pools.md`.
Standard ADR structure: Context, Decision, Consequences, Out of scope,
References.

## Decision points the ADR must resolve

1. Quota fraction → cost mapping (linear? step function? thresholds?).
2. Quota window time handling (do we reserve quota for later? probably
   not in v1 — use available now).
3. Token estimate source (the request itself? a heuristic? a learned
   model?). v1 likely uses a coarse system-prompt-length-based
   estimate.
4. Fallback chain ordering when quota exhausts mid-request (single try?
   retry on alternate?).
5. How `IncludeByDefault: false` (bead D) composes with cost ranking
   (drops from default candidate set entirely; not just deprioritized).

## In-scope files

- docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-011-cost-based-routing-with-quota-pools.md
  (new)

## Out of scope

- Implementation (separate EPIC after ADR acceptance).
- Quota signal infrastructure beyond what 7776890e already provides
  (any new signals needed get filed as their own beads under the EPIC).
- Per-tenant or per-key quota tracking (flag as future scope in the
  ADR).

title: ADR-011: Cost-based routing with quota-pool-aware fallback

description:
Amend the routing and snapshot governing docs so implementation is not relying on chat context.

Required design contract:
- Autorouting decision = route(client_inputs, fiz_models_snapshot).
- Client inputs include policy/profile, pins, no_remote, metered opt-in, tools/context/reasoning needs, and other explicit constraints.
- The fiz models snapshot is the only source of routing facts: health, quota, model availability, effective cost, actual_cash_spend, billing kind, context/tools/reasoning support, locality, reliability, latency, utilization, and per-field freshness.
- Fiz owns synchronous freshness: refresh operations use cross-process locks, single-flight/coalescing, TTLs, cooldowns, bounded concurrency, and atomic snapshot writes. Autorouting may synchronously refresh stale routing-relevant fields before scoring.
- Fiz exposes an optional async refresh/warmup entrypoint that a long-lived ddx server can call on a heartbeat. This is a cache-warming optimization; route correctness must not require ddx server.
- fiz models is quick; it reads the current snapshot and, when fields are stale, prints refresh guidance instead of starting an uncoordinated background probe. fiz models --refresh blocks on routing-relevant stale fields; fiz models --refresh-all blocks on all refreshable fields.
- Hard gates are limited to explicit user constraints and dispatchability. Cost, quality, health risk, latency, utilization, and power fit are scoring inputs.
- Subscription models use PAYG-equivalent effective/shadow cost for scoring while retaining actual_cash_spend=false. Pay-per-token providers are gated only when dispatch creates actual metered spend and the user has not opted in.

In scope: docs/helix/01-frame/features/FEAT-004-model-routing.md, docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-011-cost-based-routing-with-quota-pools.md, docs/helix/02-design/contracts/CONTRACT-003-fizeau-service.md, docs/routing/*.md, ADR-012 snapshot/cache docs if present.

Out of scope: implementation code; create follow-up beads instead of partially implementing.
title: Specify snapshot-first autorouting and freshness ownership

description:
Design ADR for the per-source on-disk cache that backs the available-
models snapshot. Defines file layout, locking semantics, single-flight
refresh algorithm, atomic-write contract, force-refresh behavior, TTLs,
crash recovery, and the mandatory test matrix for the implementation.

This is design-only. Implementation is bead M1.

## ADR scope (sections to write)

### Context
- The `fiz models` snapshot needs live data from multiple sources
  (provider /v1/models, harness PTY, /props introspection, /usage
  endpoints, rate-limit response headers).
- Discovery sources are slow (PTY ~30s, OR /api/v1/models ~316ms).
- Runtime signals (quota, status, latency) change frequently.
- UI must never hang (100ms budget).
- Multi-process: `ddx work`, `ddx try`, `fiz models`, routing all run
  concurrently and must share cache state.
- fizeau already has a battle-tested file-locking idiom in
  `cmd/bench/matrix.go:acquireMatrixLock` (PID + atomic-create-or-fail
  with crash recovery via PID-alive check). Build on it.

### Decision

#### File layout
```
~/.cache/fizeau/   (os.UserCacheDir())
├── discovery/                          # slow, stable
│   ├── openrouter.json                 # 24h TTL
│   ├── claude-subscription.json        # 24h TTL
│   ├── codex-subscription.json         # 24h TTL
│   ├── vidar-ds4.json                  # 1h TTL (local)
│   └── sindri-club-3090-llamacpp.json  # 1h TTL (local)
└── runtime/                            # hot, volatile
    ├── openrouter.json                 # 5min TTL
    ├── claude-subscription.json        # 5min TTL
    └── ...
```
Each data file is JSON. Keys map to fizeau's canonical model identity:
`<provider>/<model_id>`.

#### Lock + marker pattern (two-tier)
- `<source>.lock`: brief mutation lock. Same idiom as
  `acquireMatrixLock` — `O_CREATE|O_EXCL`, JSON `{PID, StartedAt}`,
  crash recovery via `processAlive(PID)`. Held during state
  transitions only (claim refresh, finish refresh).
- `<source>.refreshing`: long-lived refresh marker. JSON
  `{PID, StartedAt, Deadline}`. Held during refresh IO. Other
  processes see this and wait OR return stale.

#### Algorithms (full pseudocode in ADR)
1. `claim_refresh(source) -> ClaimedByMe | AlreadyInFlight(marker)`
2. `refresh_and_commit(source)` — IO, atomic-rename, marker cleanup
3. `wait_for_refresh(source, marker, max_wait)` — poll marker
4. `read(source) -> (data, fresh_bool)` — UI never blocks
5. `force_refresh(source)` — sync; wait or claim
6. In-process single-flight via `golang.org/x/sync/singleflight`
   (composes with file-based marker for cross-process)

#### TTL defaults (operator-configurable via env or config)
- Discovery PTY: 24h (PTY is slow; refresh rare)
- Discovery HTTP /v1/models: 24h
- Discovery HTTP /v1/models (local): 1h
- Runtime signals: 5 min
- Lock acquisition timeout: 100ms
- Refresh deadline: 60s for PTY, 10s for HTTP discovery, 5s for runtime
- Marker staleness threshold: 2× refresh deadline

#### Crash recovery
- Stale `.refreshing` markers (past deadline OR dead PID) are
  presumed orphan; next caller overrides. No manual cleanup needed.
- Stale `.lock` files (dead PID) are removed on next attempt
  (existing `acquireMatrixLock` semantics).

#### Force-refresh semantics
- Synchronous. Waits for in-flight refresh to complete (up to
  refresh deadline). Then reads fresh data.
- Bypasses TTL check.

#### Cache prune
- `fiz cache prune` removes data files for sources not in current
  config. Called explicitly OR auto-pruned at startup (operator
  decision; ADR proposes manual to avoid surprises).

### Mandatory test matrix (10 tests)

The implementation bead (M1) MUST ship all 10 tests passing. Skipping
any = the cache is dangerous. Spawn child Go processes for multi-
process scenarios (standard `TestMain` + `os.Args[0]` -test.run
helper-process pattern).

1. **Concurrent claim from 2 procs**: exactly one ClaimedByMe.
2. **Reader during refresh**: atomic rename → no torn read; reader
   sees old until rename, new after.
3. **Crash during refresh** (kill -9): next process detects dead PID
   in marker, claims.
4. **Refresh timeout**: marker deadline expires, next process
   claims; orig process's late commit rejected (lock check).
5. **Force-refresh waits + reads fresh**.
6. **Concurrent normal+force**: normal returns stale immediately,
   background refresh single-flighted with force.
7. **Atomic rename verified**: 100 concurrent readers + 1 writer
   producing 100 versions; every read is a complete consistent
   version (checksum).
8. **`fiz cache prune` doesn't race active sources**.
9. **Stale-while-revalidate**: stale read returns immediately,
   background refresh fires.
10. **PID reuse safety**: marker PID gets reused by unrelated
    process; deadline check is the safety net.

### Consequences

**Positive:**
- UI never hangs (read path is always cache-bounded < 100ms).
- Multi-process safe across `ddx work` and `fiz` invocations.
- Crash-safe via PID-alive + deadline checks.
- Reuses fizeau's proven `acquireMatrixLock` idiom; no new
  cross-platform lock library required.
- Each source has independent lifecycle; OR re-discovery doesn't
  block PTY discovery.

**Negative:**
- Two file roles per source (lock + marker) is more complexity
  than a single mutex.
- File-based coordination has inherent edge cases (PID reuse,
  filesystem semantics differences); test matrix is non-trivial.
- Stale data may be served briefly during refresh (acceptable
  trade for non-blocking UI).

### Out of scope
- Persistent storage of historical signals (cache is per-source,
  current-state only).
- Multi-machine cache sharing (per-host only).
- Compression (JSON, plain).
- Encryption at rest.
- OR sub-provider cache (deferred M5).

### References
- Bead A (`fizeau-b2c5c826`) — version-aware ranker, parses model IDs
- Bead D (`fizeau-d18e11f5`) — IncludeByDefault filter (composes with
  AutoRoutable in snapshot)
- Bead E1 (`fizeau-c04be6b0`) — quota_pool catalog field (consumed in
  M2 enrichment)
- Bead E2 (`fizeau-5b6512ef`) — ADR-011 cost-based routing (downstream
  consumer of snapshot)
- `cmd/bench/matrix.go:acquireMatrixLock` — prior-art lock idiom
- `internal/harnesses/{claude,codex}/quota_cache.go` — atomic-rename
  prior art

## In-scope files
- docs/helix/02-design/adr/ADR-012-per-source-on-disk-cache.md (new)

## Out of scope
- Implementation (bead M1).
title: ADR-012: Per-source on-disk cache for discovery + runtime signals

Misconfigured fixture sweep exits code 75 after exactly 5 cells with aborted.json written. 3. --no-consecutive-failure-halt runs all cells without aborting. 4. lane_aborted appears as a known invalid class in matrix.json's invalid_by_class breakdown.
description:
Add a consecutive-failure halt to the matrix runner (cmd/bench/matrix.go) that aborts the entire lane after N=5 cells fail with byte-identical error fingerprints in a row. Writes a single `lane_aborted` summary cell explaining why; subsequent cells in the lane are skipped (not run, not written).

Why this matters: see parent epic. The preflight smoke test (sibling bead fizeau-586e476a) catches misconfigured lanes at sweep startup. This bead is the defense-in-depth piece for cases where preflight passed but the endpoint dies mid-sweep — e.g. vLLM model engine enters a degraded state after running fine for a while, or a model server gets OOM-killed and the wrapper restarts it broken. Today such regressions cause every remaining cell in the lane to fail identically until human intervention.

Behavior:

- After each cell in a lane completes, compute a fingerprint = SHA256 of the report's `error` field (truncated to first 256 bytes) + `final_status`. Store the last N=5 fingerprints in lane-scoped state inside the existing per-lane matrix runner process (no on-disk state).
- When 5 consecutive cells share the same fingerprint AND are all `graded_fail` or `harness_crash` (not `graded_pass`), halt the lane:
  * Write one synthetic `report.json` to a path like `<cells_root>/.lane_aborted/<lane_id>/aborted.json` with `final_status: "lane_aborted"`, `error: "halted after 5 consecutive identical failures: <fingerprint preview>"`, plus the fingerprint and the list of the 5 task ids that triggered.
  * Print `[matrix] lane=<lane_id> ABORTED after 5 consecutive identical failures (last task: <task>): <error preview>` to stderr.
  * Exit the matrix runner with a distinct exit code (e.g. 75 / EX_TEMPFAIL) so the sweep script can attribute the abort to this cause vs a normal lane completion.
- Add `final_status: "lane_aborted"` to the matrix invalid-class machinery so the website / aggregates display these explicitly (not as silent missing cells).
- Add a `--no-consecutive-failure-halt` flag to disable the safety net for operators debugging a known-broken lane.

Failure-mode test cases (must all be covered):

- 5 cells with identical `agent: provider error: ...` errors → halt fires.
- 5 cells with identical `harness_crash: context canceled` (the docker subnet exhaustion pattern from earlier in this codebase) → halt fires.
- 4 identical errors then 1 graded_pass → halt does NOT fire; counter resets.
- 4 identical errors then a 5th error with even one byte different → halt does NOT fire.
- `--no-consecutive-failure-halt` flag → halt never fires regardless.

In-scope files:
- cmd/bench/matrix.go — add ConsecutiveFailureTracker (or inline state) inside the matrix-runner main loop; integrate into the per-cell write path; add the flag.
- cmd/bench/matrix_invalid.go — register `lane_aborted` as a known invalid class (so summarizeMatrixCells counts it correctly, and the regen pipeline treats it as systemic, not model failure).
- cmd/bench/matrix_test.go — add the four behavior tests above using the existing test fixture pattern.

Out-of-scope (file separate beads):
- Auto-restart of the lane after the user fixes the issue. (For now, operator manually re-runs.)
- Cross-lane abort cascade (one lane's repeated failures shouldn't halt other lanes).
- Surfacing the halt count in the website data — that's a follow-up once we see how often this fires in practice.

Acceptance is a command:

1. `go test ./cmd/bench/... -run TestConsecutiveFailureHalt` passes (covers the four behaviors above).
2. With a deliberately-misconfigured fixture profile that produces identical errors, `fiz-bench matrix --profiles broken-fixture --reps 10 --out /tmp/abort-test` exits with code 75 after exactly 5 cells, writes `aborted.json` under `<out>/.lane_aborted/`, and the remaining 5 cells are not present in the output dir.
3. The same fixture with `--no-consecutive-failure-halt` runs all 10 cells to completion (each failing) without aborting.
title: bench: matrix runner halts lane after 5 consecutive identical failures

description:
Extend the fiz models snapshot data model so every fact needed by routing is visible to operators and fixture tests.

The snapshot must represent, per candidate/model row where applicable:
- billing kind: subscription, metered/per-token, fixed/local, test, or unknown
- actual_cash_spend: whether dispatching this candidate creates incremental cash spend
- effective_cost: comparable routing cost used for scoring, including PAYG-equivalent shadow cost for subscription models
- effective_cost_source/provenance
- health state and freshness timestamp/source
- quota state and freshness timestamp/source
- model availability/discovery freshness
- context/tools/reasoning support
- locality, reliability, latency, utilization signals when known

Root cause: subscription-backed models currently look equally included for scoring, so cheap can pick maximum-quality frontier subscription models. The model snapshot must carry comparable effective cost separately from actual cash spend so routing can prefer the cheapest sufficiently capable route without hidden probes or if/else frontier bans.

In scope: model snapshot structs, assembler/enrichment code, JSON output schema, tests and fixtures.

Out of scope: changing route scoring; changing CLI refresh behavior; server refresh orchestration.
title: Expose routing cost and freshness facts in the models snapshot

description:
Build the in-memory `ModelSnapshot` view by enumerating all configured
providers (incl harnesses-as-providers), reading per-source caches
(via M1), parsing model IDs via the version-aware ranker (bead A), and
joining with catalog metadata (power, cost, reasoning_levels,
quota_pool from bead E1).

## Public API sketch

```go
package modelregistry

type Source string
const (
    SourceNativeAPI    Source = "native_api"
    SourceHarnessPTY   Source = "harness_pty"
    SourcePropsAPI     Source = "props_api"
)

type ModelStatus string
const (
    StatusAvailable     ModelStatus = "available"
    StatusRateLimited   ModelStatus = "rate_limited"
    StatusUnreachable   ModelStatus = "unreachable"
    StatusUnknown       ModelStatus = "unknown"
)

type KnownModel struct {
    // Identity (per ADR-012; matches ModelRef)
    Provider      string
    ID            string

    // How fizeau learned of it
    DiscoveredVia Source
    DiscoveredAt  time.Time

    // Parsed via bead A
    Family        string
    Version       []int
    Tier          modelcatalog.Tier
    PreRelease    bool

    // From catalog join
    Power            int
    CostInputPerM    float64
    CostOutputPerM   float64
    ContextWindow    int
    ReasoningLevels  []string
    QuotaPool        string  // E1

    // Composed eligibility (consumed by routing)
    AutoRoutable    bool      // catalog × IncludeByDefault × Status
    ExclusionReason string
}

type ModelSnapshot struct {
    Models  []KnownModel
    AsOf    time.Time
    Sources map[string]SourceMeta
}

type SourceMeta struct {
    LastRefreshedAt time.Time
    Stale           bool
    Error           string
}

func Assemble(ctx context.Context, cfg *config.Config, cat *modelcatalog.Catalog, cache *discoverycache.Cache) (ModelSnapshot, error)
```

## Provider enumeration

Iterates `cfg.Providers` (existing user config) plus implicit
subscription harnesses (claude-subscription, codex-subscription) when
their CLIs are installed and authenticated.

## Per-source discovery

Each provider type has a discovery adapter:
- openai-compat (openrouter, openai, vidar-ds4, sindri-llamacpp): GET
  `/v1/models` via cache
- ds4: also GET `/props`
- claude / codex harnesses: PTY-based cassette read via cache (existing
  model_discovery.go code, now wrapped through M1's cache)

Adapters return `[]string` (model IDs); assembler produces
`KnownModel` per ID.

## Tier ranker integration

Each model ID parsed via `modelcatalog.tiers.Parse(id)` (bead A). Sets
Family/Version/Tier/PreRelease.

## Catalog join

For each (provider, model) candidate, look up the catalog entry. If
present, populate Power/Cost/ContextWindow/ReasoningLevels/QuotaPool.
If not, leave zero/empty (snapshot still includes the entry —
provider-discovered models without catalog metadata are still
routable, just with limited info).

## AutoRoutable composition

`AutoRoutable = catalog.AutoRoutable() AND providerConfig.IncludeByDefault
AND status != StatusUnreachable AND status != StatusUnknown`. (Per
bead D's `IncludeByDefault` filter wiring.)

## Catalog-only suppression

Per operator direction: if a catalog model is NOT discovered by any
configured provider, it does NOT appear in the snapshot. The catalog
is implementation detail.

## In-scope files

- internal/modelregistry/types.go — KnownModel, ModelSnapshot, etc.
- internal/modelregistry/assemble.go — top-level Assemble function
- internal/modelregistry/discovery.go — per-provider-type adapters
  (uses existing code in internal/provider/openai/discovery.go and
  internal/harnesses/{claude,codex}/model_discovery.go through M1's
  cache wrapper)
- internal/modelregistry/enrich.go — catalog join + AutoRoutable
- internal/modelregistry/assemble_test.go
- internal/modelregistry/discovery_test.go
- internal/modelregistry/enrich_test.go

## Depends on

- M0 (ADR-012) accepted
- M1 (cache + ParseModelRef) implemented
- Bead A (`fizeau-b2c5c826`, version-aware ranker)
- Bead E1 (`fizeau-c04be6b0`, quota_pool field)

## Out of scope

- Runtime signal collection (M3 — Status comes from M3's runtime cache).
- CLI (M4).
- OR sub-provider expansion (M5).

description:
PROBLEM
Fizeau wastes 2m45s per call retrying a dead local endpoint (4 attempts with 1s/2s/4s/8s backoff + per-attempt 30s dial timeout), then surfaces a provider_connectivity error to the caller. Even when the caller (DDx) records the failed (provider, model) and asks for re-routing on the next attempt, Fizeau re-routes to the same dead endpoint because the caller has no way to express "exclude this (provider, model)" in the request, and routing decisions are made in isolation from caller-side health signals. Observed reproduction: with bragi:1234/qwen3.5-27b unreachable, a DDx drain of 12 beads attempted each bead, every bead routed to bragi/qwen3.5-27b, every bead spent ~2m45s in Fizeau retries, every bead failed identically. Net drain time ~33 minutes, zero beads completed.

ROOT CAUSE
Two contributing behaviors in internal/provider/* and internal/serviceimpl/*:
1. Connect-time errors (dial tcp ECONNREFUSED / ETIMEDOUT to a local IP) are treated the same as in-flight provider errors and run through the full retry curve (4 attempts × ~30s = 2m45s). A local-network dial that misses the SYN window will not succeed on subsequent attempts; the retry burns wall-clock for no value.
2. The route decision in internal/routehealth and the public RouteRequest type carry no caller-supplied exclusion list. Routes recorded as failed by the caller cannot be expressed to Fizeau. The internal routehealth Store tracks process-local feedback only — fine for within-call retries, but stateless across calls.

PROPOSED FIX
- Add ExcludedRoutes []ExcludedRoute to the public RouteRequest, where ExcludedRoute names (Provider, Model, optional Endpoint). The router must skip any candidate matching the list. Document in service.go and the routing API doc.
- For connect-time errors (errors.As to *net.OpError with Op=="dial" AND ECONNREFUSED/ETIMEDOUT/EHOSTUNREACH), bypass the retry curve: 1 attempt, fail fast within ~5s. Reserve the retry curve for in-flight 5xx / partial-response errors that may transient retry usefully.
- When the request lists multiple viable endpoints for the same model (current or future endpoint-first config), on connect-failure to one endpoint, immediately try the next endpoint in the same call rather than surfacing failure. The order matches the existing route-decision strategy.
- Expose a routing-quality event recording "route excluded by caller hint" so observability can distinguish caller-driven re-routes from internal health signals.

NON-SCOPE
- Do not redesign provider config syntax. ExcludedRoutes is additive; endpoint-list support can land separately.
- Do not change the existing routehealth in-process Store contract; only extend RouteRequest.
- Do not alter retry behavior for non-transport-class provider errors (5xx, rate limit, content-policy) in this bead.

DEPS
None at Fizeau; DDx-side bead to actually populate ExcludedRoutes from execute-loop-failed-routes is filed separately under ddx (ddx-8a7a6843 covers the DDx escalation-signaling side).
title: routing: honor caller exclusions and fast-fail on transport timeouts

description:
Implement the per-source on-disk cache per ADR-012. New package
`internal/discoverycache/`. Foundation for M2 (snapshot assembler) and
M3 (runtime signals); no consumers in this bead.

Also includes the canonical model identity helpers (`ModelRef`,
`ParseModelRef`) since they're the cache's primary key.

## In-scope files

- internal/discoverycache/cache.go — public API (Get, ForceRefresh,
  WriteRefresh, Prune)
- internal/discoverycache/lock.go — mutation lock (extends
  acquireMatrixLock idiom; PID+atomic-create with crash recovery)
- internal/discoverycache/marker.go — refresh marker (PID+StartedAt+
  Deadline; staleness detection)
- internal/discoverycache/atomic_write.go — tmp+fsync+rename helper
- internal/discoverycache/singleflight.go — composes
  golang.org/x/sync/singleflight with file marker for cross-process
- internal/discoverycache/cache_test.go — basic single-process tests
- internal/discoverycache/multiprocess_test.go — the 10 mandatory
  tests using `TestHelperProcess` / child-spawn pattern
- internal/modelref/ref.go — canonical identity helpers
  (ModelRef struct, Parse, String, validation rules)
- internal/modelref/ref_test.go — table tests for parser

## Algorithms to implement (per ADR-012)

All 6 from ADR-012:
1. claim_refresh(source) → (ClaimedByMe | AlreadyInFlight(marker))
2. refresh_and_commit(source, dataBytes) — atomic rename + marker
   cleanup
3. wait_for_refresh(source, marker, maxWait) — polling
4. read(source) → (data, age, fresh_bool)
5. force_refresh(source, refresher func()) — sync
6. In-process singleflight composition — use
   golang.org/x/sync/singleflight, composes with file-marker

## Public API sketch

```go
type Cache struct {
    Root string  // e.g. ~/.cache/fizeau
}

type Source struct {
    Tier            string  // "discovery" or "runtime"
    Name            string  // e.g. "openrouter", "claude-subscription"
    TTL             time.Duration
    RefreshDeadline time.Duration
}

type ReadResult struct {
    Data       []byte
    Age        time.Duration
    Fresh      bool  // true if Age < TTL
    Stale      bool  // true if Age >= TTL
}

func (c *Cache) Read(s Source) (ReadResult, error)

// Refresher is the source-fetch function. Cache handles single-flight
// + atomic write + marker lifecycle.
type Refresher func(ctx context.Context) ([]byte, error)

func (c *Cache) Refresh(s Source, fn Refresher) error          // sync, force
func (c *Cache) MaybeRefresh(s Source, fn Refresher)           // async, no-op if fresh
func (c *Cache) Prune(activeSources []Source) error             // remove unknown
```

## Canonical identity (`internal/modelref`)

```go
type ModelRef struct {
    Provider string  // slug: lowercase, alphanumeric, hyphens, dots
    ID       string  // provider's canonical model ID; case-preserved
}

func Parse(s string) (ModelRef, error)  // split on first /
func (r ModelRef) String() string         // <provider>/<id>
func (r ModelRef) Validate() error        // empty checks, char rules
```

Edge cases per ADR / discussion:
- `openrouter/qwen/qwen3.6-27b` → provider=openrouter, id=qwen/qwen3.6-27b
- `vidar-ds4/deepseek-v4-flash` → provider=vidar-ds4, id=deepseek-v4-flash
- Empty either side → error
- Provider name with slash → error
- Multiple slashes → split on first only
- Future M5: `@<sub_provider>` suffix is a separate concern, not in this bead

## Mandatory test matrix (per ADR-012)

ALL 10 tests in `multiprocess_test.go` MUST pass:

1. concurrent claim → exactly one ClaimedByMe
2. reader during refresh → atomic rename, no torn read
3. crash during refresh (kill -9 child) → next process claims via dead-PID
4. refresh timeout → marker deadline expires, claim
5. force-refresh waits + returns fresh
6. concurrent normal+force → singleflight composition
7. atomic rename: 100 readers + 1 writer × 100 versions, no torn read
8. cache prune doesn't race active sources
9. stale-while-revalidate: stale returns immediately + bg refresh fires
10. PID reuse safety: deadline check is the fallback

Use `TestHelperProcess` pattern (Go stdlib pattern; spawn `os.Args[0]
-test.run=TestHelperProcess --` with custom args).

## Out of scope

- M2 (snapshot assembler) — uses this cache.
- M3 (runtime signals) — uses this cache.
- M4 (CLI) — top-level surface.
- Any actual provider/harness fetch logic — Refresher is injected.

## Depends on

- ADR-012 (M0) accepted before this bead executes (uses its design
  contract).

description:
The matrix runner aborts a lane after 5 consecutive identical failures (cmd/bench/matrix.go:36 matrixConsecutiveFailureLimit). Today's sindri-lucebox sweep died this way: the lucebox endpoint stopped serving mid-sweep, every cell hit the same connection error, the abort fingerprint (sha256 of Error[:256] + FinalStatus) collapsed to the same hash across 5 cells, and the lane was killed even though the underlying server was momentarily down (and recoverable).

Fix: classify connection-class / HTTP 5xx / EOF / parse errors as transient. Transient errors (a) do not count toward the consecutive-failure halt counter, (b) trigger an indefinite per-cell retry loop with exponential backoff capped at 60s, interruptible via parent context (ctrl-C / SIGTERM). HTTP 429 is intentionally NOT classified as transient — rate-limits are persistent enough that an indefinite retry can stall the sweep, surface them as failures so they're visible and tunable at the resource-group / sampling level. New CLI flags --no-cell-retry (default off) and --cell-retry-backoff-max (default 60s).
title: bench/matrix: retry transient errors instead of aborting the lane
